entries.title: A Comprehensive Guide to Differential Privacy: From Theory to User Expectations
entries.subtitle: A comprehensive review of differential privacy frameworks, mechanisms, and applications for mitigating privacy risks in data-driven research.
entries.abstract: This survey provides a comprehensive examination of differential privacy (DP) as a mathematically grounded framework for addressing privacy concerns in data-intensive applications. The review covers theoretical foundations, practical mechanisms, and real-world applications across domains including machine learning and synthetic data generation. The paper identifies key algorithmic tools, domain-specific challenges, and usability issues, with emphasis on improving communication and transparency in DP system implementation to support responsible data use practices.

entries.title: The Constitutional Way of Thinking
entries.abstract: This paper examines several elements of "the constitutional way of thinking" beginning with a discussion of the commonality problem in patterns of thinking. Later sections address the productivity of reductionist classification schemes and how this is usefully applied to politics. More specifically, the discussion focuses first on why economists, in particular, have difficulty with this approach; second on the general problem of adhering to idealist thinking in political theory; and third on speculation into the conditions necessary to acquire "the constitutional way of thinking."

entries.title: Reranking Social Media Feeds: A Practical Guide for Field Experiments
entries.abstract: Social media plays a central role in shaping public opinion and behavior, yet performing experiments on these platforms and, in particular, on feed algorithms is becoming increasingly challenging. This guide offers practical recommendations for researchers developing and deploying field experiments focused on real-time reranking of social media feeds. The article is organized around two contributions. First, we provide an overview of an experimental method using web browser extensions that intercepts and reranks content in real time, enabling naturalistic reranking field experiments. We then describe feed interventions and measurements that this paradigm enables on participants' actual feeds, without requiring the involvement of social media platforms. Second, we offer concrete technical recommendations for intercepting and reranking social media feeds with minimal user-facing delay, and provide an open-source implementation. This document aims to summarize lessons learned in running field experiments on social media, provide concrete implementation details, and foster the ecosystem of independent social media research. Finally, we release the source code that serves as a blueprint for implementing future feed-ranking experiments.

entries.title: The Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal
entries.subtitle: An examination of the logical incompatibility between individual liberty and the Pareto principle in social choice theory.
entries.abstract: This paper presents an impossibility result demonstrating fundamental tensions between liberal principles of individual liberty and conventional social choice mechanisms. The authors formalize a minimalist concept of individual libertyÃ¢ÂÂthe notion that individuals should retain absolute freedom over personal decisions regardless of majority preferenceÃ¢ÂÂand prove its incompatibility with basic economic efficiency principles. The analysis reveals deep structural conflicts in constructing social decision rules that simultaneously respect both individual autonomy and collective welfare optimization.

entries.title: Social Choice and Individual Values, 1st ed.
entries.subtitle: An examination of the formal conditions required for aggregating individual preferences into rational collective social choices.
entries.publisher: Cowles Foundation for Research in Economics
entries.abstract: This text investigates whether it is formally possible to construct a procedure for transforming individual preferences into coherent social decision-making that satisfies basic rationality conditions. The author examines four methods of social choiceÃ¢ÂÂvoting, market mechanisms, dictatorship, and conventionÃ¢ÂÂand demonstrates through the paradox of voting that majority preference aggregation fails to produce transitive collective preferences. The study questions whether any method of aggregating diverse individual tastes can produce rational collective behavior, establishing foundational questions for social choice theory.

entries.title: DNA as a universal chemical substrate for computing and data storage
entries.abstract: DNA computing and DNA data storage represent emerging paradigms that leverage DNA molecules as computing substrates and storage media, offering nanoscale compactness and operation in unconventional aqueous environments. This review explores the integration of DNA computing with neural networks and compartmentalized DNA circuits, as well as strategies for writing, reading, retrieving, and editing DNA-encoded data. The work bridges DNA computing and data storage fields and examines applications of DNA-based near-memory computing for future information technology and health analysis.

entries.title: A Definition of AGI
entries.subtitle: Introduces a quantifiable framework for AGI grounded in Cattell-Horn-Carroll theory, defining AGI as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.
entries.abstract: The lack of a concrete definition for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) obscures the gap between today's specialized AI and human-level cognition. This paper introduces a quantifiable framework to address this, defining AGI as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult. To operationalize this, we ground our methodology in Cattell-Horn-Carroll theory, the most empirically validated model of human cognition. The framework dissects general intelligence into ten core cognitive domains ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ including reasoning, memory, and perception ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂ and adapts established human psychometric batteries to rigorously evaluate AI systems. Applying this framework, we find that today's frontier AI models exhibit high but uneven performance, with notable weaknesses in specific cognitive domains.

entries.title: Audits Under Resource, Data, and Access Constraints: Scaling Laws For Less Discriminatory Alternatives
entries.subtitle: Examines how auditors can identify less discriminatory algorithmic alternatives under realistic constraints on data, compute, and model access.
entries.abstract: AI audits play a critical role in AI accountability and safety. One branch of the law for which AI audits are particularly salient is anti-discrimination law. Several areas of anti-discrimination law implicate the 'less discriminatory alternative' (LDA) requirement, in which a defendant may be liable if a similarly effective but less discriminatory alternative exists. This paper examines how auditors can identify less discriminatory alternatives under realistic constraints on data, compute, and model access, and derives scaling laws governing the relationship between audit resources and the probability of successfully identifying such alternatives.

entries.title: On Goodhart's Law, with an Application to Value Alignment
entries.subtitle: Formalizes Goodhart's Law and applies it to the value alignment problem, showing that optimizing proxy measures of human values tends to undermine the values themselves.
entries.abstract: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." This paper formalizes Goodhart's Law and applies it to the problem of value alignment in AI systems. The authors show that when an AI system is trained to optimize a proxy measure of human values, the resulting system tends to undermine the values the proxy was meant to capture. The paper derives conditions under which alignment by proxy fails and discusses implications for the design of reward functions and evaluation frameworks.

entries.title: The Coasean Singularity? Demand, Supply, and Market Design with AI Agents
entries.subtitle: Applies Coasean theory of the firm to AI agents, arguing that sufficiently capable agents collapse transaction costs and reorganize markets around new equilibria.
entries.abstract: AI agentsÃ¢ÂÂautonomous systems that perceive, reason, and act on behalf of human principalsÃ¢ÂÂare poised to transform digital markets by dramatically reducing transaction costs. This chapter evaluates the economic implications of this transition, adopting a consumer-oriented view of agents as market participants that can search, negotiate, and transact directly. From the demand side, agent adoption reflects derived demand: users trade off decision quality against effort reduction, with outcomes mediated by agent capability and task context. On the supply side, firms will design, integrate, and monetize agents, with outcomes hinging on whether agents operate within or across platforms. At the market level, agents create efficiency gains from lower search, communication, and contracting costs, but also introduce frictions such as congestion and price obfuscation. By lowering the costs of preference elicitation, contract enforcement, and identity verification, agents expand the feasible set of market designs but also raise novel regulatory challenges. While the net welfare effects remain an empirical question, the rapid onset of AI-mediated transactions presents a unique opportunity for economic research to inform real-world policy and market design.

entries.title: An Economy of AI Agents
entries.subtitle: Surveys how AI agents capable of planning and executing complex tasks may reshape markets, organizations, and the institutions required for well-functioning economies.
entries.abstract: In the coming decade, artificially intelligent agents with the ability to plan and execute complex tasks over long time horizons with little direct oversight from humans may be deployed across the economy. This chapter surveys recent developments and highlights open questions for economists around how AI agents might interact with humans and with each other, shape markets and organizations, and what institutions might be required for well-functioning markets.

entries.title: A Note on Human Capital, the Division of Labor, and Artificial Intelligence
entries.subtitle: Argues that AI has a comparative advantage in coordination tasks, which deepens human specialization rather than replacing specialists.
entries.publisher: Peter Isztin / Corvinus University of Economics
entries.abstract: This paper considers the effects of generative artificial intelligence tools on the division of labor. Following Becker and Murphy (1992), the author posits a team with each member assigned to a task. Specialization is limited not only by the extent of the market, but also by coordination costs, which include the need for knowledge in multiple tasks. AI helps the coordination of the team by being a substitute for human generalist knowledge, resulting in a greater number of specialized fields. The introduction of AI also increases the return to knowledge that is equally productive in all tasks. The paper shows that AI agents would typically have a comparative advantage in coordinating specialists, while human agents perform specialist roles.
